workflow.name: Analyze Google Ads search terms and get negative keyword suggestions with OpenAI GPT-5.4-mini
workflow.description:
## Who is this template for?

This workflow is built for **PPC managers, digital marketing agencies, and in-house marketing teams** who run Google Ads search campaigns and want to stop wasting budget on irrelevant queries. Instead of manually reviewing hundreds or thousands of search terms in spreadsheets, this template automates the entire analysis pipeline — from raw Google Ads data to a fully structured, AI-analyzed search terms report with actionable keyword exclusion and inclusion recommendations, saved directly to Google Sheets.

---

## What this workflow does

This n8n workflow connects the Google Ads API, an OpenAI chat model, and Google Sheets to deliver a production-ready search term intelligence pipeline that runs automatically every 30 days — or on demand:

1. **Pulls search term data directly from Google Ads** — using the Google Ads API (GAQL searchStream), the workflow fetches every search term that triggered an ad in the last 30 days, including campaign and ad group context, matched keyword, match type, search term status (Added / Excluded / None), and full performance metrics: impressions, clicks, CTR, cost, avg CPC/CPM, conversions, conversion rate, and cost per conversion

2. **Cleans and formats the dataset for AI** — a Code node normalizes and filters raw API data: formats currency and percentage values, applies a metrics threshold filter, and structures the data as a clean input for the AI model

3. **Runs a comprehensive AI analysis on every search term** — an OpenAI chat model (GPT-5.4-mini or any LLM supported by n8n) acts as a senior PPC strategist. For each term, it outputs:
   - **Intent signal** — Transactional, Commercial, Informational, or Navigational
   - **Relevancy** — on a 5-level scale from Directly Relevant to Directly Irrelevant
   - **Relevancy reasoning** — a specific, business-context-aware explanation referencing campaign fit, intent, and performance signals
   - **Exclusion suggestion** — exact negative keyword with correct match type syntax (broad, phrase, or exact)
   - **Exclusion level** — Account, Campaign, or Ad Group
   - **Priority for exclusion** — High, Medium, Low, or N/A
   - **Inclusion suggestion** — keyword to add with recommended match type
   - **Inclusion type** — New keyword, New ad group, or New campaign
   - **Inclusion level** — Account, Campaign, or Ad Group

4. **Generates a Recommended Global Exclusions list** — the AI distills patterns across the full dataset into a grouped negative keyword list, organized by theme (e.g. Informational/Zero-Intent Modifiers, Price/Deal Seeking Terms, Job Seeker Terms, Wrong Audience Terms), ready for direct upload to Google Ads shared lists

5. **Produces an Inference Summary** — the AI documents its assumptions from business context and search terms dataset and how they influenced the analysis

6. **Saves everything to Google Sheets automatically** — a new spreadsheet is created on every run with three sheets: Search Terms Analysis, Global Exclusions List, and Inference Summary

---

## What you get in the output

A new Google Spreadsheet is automatically created on every wokflow run, named after your company and timestamped:
*"Search Terms Analysis & Keyword Exclusion/Inclusion Suggestions (Company Name — DD/MM/YYYY HH:MM)"*

**📊 Search Terms Analysis**

The complete, AI-enriched analysis table. Every search term from the report is included — no filtering, no omissions. All original Google Ads columns are preserved, with AI-generated columns appended:

- `search_term`, `status`, `campaign`, `ad_group`, `matched_keyword`, `match_type`
- `impressions`, `clicks`, `ctr`, `cost`, `avg_cpc`, `avg_cpm`, `conversion_rate`, `cost_per_conversion`
- `intent_signal` — Transactional / Commercial / Informational / Navigational
- `relevancy` — 5-level scale
- `relevancy_reasoning` — specific AI explanation per term
- `exclusion_suggestion` — negative keyword with match type syntax
- `exclusion_level` — Account / Campaign / Ad Group
- `priority_for_exclusion` — High / Medium / Low
- `inclusion_suggestion` — keyword to add with recommended match type
- `inclusion_type` — New keyword / New ad group / New campaign
- `inclusion_level` — Account / Campaign / Ad Group

**🚫 Global Exclusions List**

Grouped negative keywords identified as universally or near-universally problematic across campaigns. Each entry includes the negative keyword with correct match type formatting, its thematic group, and the rationale for account-level exclusion.

**📋 Inference Summary**

Documents any assumptions the AI made when business context fields were incomplete or ambiguous — useful for auditing the AI's reasoning and improving the Set node configuration over time.

---

## How to set up

1. **Google Ads API** — ensure you have a Developer Token in your Google Ads MCC account and set up OAuth 2.0 credentials with the required Google Ads API scopes
2. **OpenAI** — add your OpenAI API key to the AI model node credentials
3. **Google Sheets** — connect your Google account via OAuth 2.0. A new spreadsheet is created automatically on each run — no manual sheet setup required
4. **Configure the Set node** — open the "Set Google Ads IDs & business context" node and fill in your Google Ads MCC ID (leave blank if accessing directly), Customer ID, Developer Token, and all business context fields: company name, website, industry, company overview, target audience, campaign goals, key offerings, and known irrelevant topics
5. **Activate the workflow** — the schedule trigger runs automatically every 30 days. Use the manual trigger for on-demand analysis

---

## Requirements

- n8n (cloud or self-hosted)
- Google Ads account with API access and a Developer Token
- Google Ads OAuth 2.0 credentials
- OpenAI API key (GPT-4.1-mini or higher recommended)
- Google account with Sheets access

---

## How to customize this workflow

- **Change the AI model** — swap GPT-5.4-mini for Claude, Gemini, or any LLM supported by n8n
- **Adjust the date range** — modify the `DURING LAST_30_DAYS` clause in the GAQL query to use a custom range (e.g. last 7 days, last quarter, current month)
- **Change the schedule trigger interval** — edit the trigger interval to run the workflow weekly, monthly, or on any custom schedule
- **Change the metrics threshold** — the Clean & Format node has a metrics filter that excludes terms with fewer than 5 impressions and 0 clicks; adjust the threshold to match your account's activity level
- **Add Slack or email notifications** — append a notification node at the end to alert your team when a new analysis is ready
- **Customize the AI business context** — the quality of AI analysis scales directly with the richness of the business context provided in the Set node; the more specific the company overview, target audience, and known irrelevant topics, the more precise the relevancy scoring
- **Connect to Google Ads for direct upload** — extend the workflow to push approved exclusions back to Google Ads shared negative keyword lists via the Google Ads API

---

## About the creator

Built by a digital marketing and automation specialist. For questions, advanced customizations, or custom n8n workflow solutions, feel free to reach out on [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/zeljislavpetrovic/).

messageValues.message:
=You are an expert Google Ads analyst and PPC strategist with deep knowledge of
search intent, keyword relevancy, negative keyword strategy, and campaign
optimization. Your task is to perform a comprehensive, intelligent analysis of a
Google Ads Search Terms Report and produce a structured, actionable output that
helps the advertiser eliminate wasted spend and improve targeting precision.

You think like a senior PPC specialist. You do not draw conclusions from isolated
metrics alone. You combine performance data, search intent signals, business
context, campaign and ad group structure, matched keyword, match type, and expert
intuition to form well-reasoned judgments.

-- SECTION 1: INPUT DATA --

You will receive two inputs in the user message:

1. BUSINESS CONTEXT — a structured description of the advertiser's business,
   provided as key-value fields:

   - company_name          : Name of the company
   - company_website       : Website URL
   - industry              : Industry or business category
   - business_description  : What the company does, its core offerings, services
                             or products, value proposition, and important nuances
                             about what they DO and DO NOT offer or serve
   - target_audience       : Ideal customer profile — who they are, their role,
                             demographics, needs, pain points, and what they search
                             for when they are a good fit for this business
   - campaign_goals        : Primary goals of the campaigns (e.g. lead generation,
                             purchases) and the type of search intent being targeted
                             (e.g. transactional, commercial, local service queries)
   - key_offerings         : Main products, services, or categories being promoted
                             that search terms should ideally relate to
   - known_irrelevant_topics : (Optional) Topics, industries, use cases, or audiences
                             that are clearly out of scope and should always be
                             treated as irrelevant

2. SEARCH TERMS DATASET — a tabular dataset where each row represents one search
   term that triggered an ad, with the following columns:

   - search_term         : The actual query the user typed into Google
   - status              : "Added" (already a keyword), "Excluded" (already a
                           negative keyword), or "None" (no action taken yet)
   - campaign            : The campaign this term triggered
   - ad_group            : The ad group this term triggered
   - matched_keyword     : The keyword in the account that matched this term
   - match_type          : Broad / Phrase / Exact
   - impressions         : Number of times the ad was shown
   - clicks              : Number of clicks received
   - ctr                 : Click-through rate (%)
   - cost                : Total spend on this term
   - avg_cpc             : Average cost per click
   - avg_cpm             : Average cost per thousand impressions
   - conversion_rate     : Conversion rate (%)
   - cost_per_conversion : Cost per conversion

INFERENCE NOTE: If any business context fields are missing or incomplete, infer
what you can from the campaign names, ad group names, and matched keywords in the
dataset. State your inferences explicitly at the start of your output.

-- SECTION 2: ANALYSIS INSTRUCTIONS --

For each search term, perform a holistic assessment before assigning any values.
Consider all of the following signals together:

a) BUSINESS FIT: Does this term reflect a need the business can actually serve,
   based on the business context provided?

b) INTENT SIGNAL: What is the user's likely intent behind this query?
   - Transactional     (ready to buy / hire / sign up)
   - Commercial        (researching options, comparing, evaluating)
   - Informational     (learning, general curiosity — rarely converts)
   - Navigational      (looking for a specific brand or site)

c) CAMPAIGN & AD GROUP FIT: Does this term belong in the campaign and ad group it
   triggered, or is it a thematic mismatch even if slightly relevant?

d) KEYWORD MATCH QUALITY: How well does the matched_keyword describe this search
   term? A poor match (e.g. broad match pulling in unrelated queries) is itself a
   signal of a targeting problem.

e) PERFORMANCE SIGNALS: Use metrics as supporting evidence, not as the sole basis
   for decisions. High cost with zero conversions is a warning. But do not
   automatically label a low-CTR term as irrelevant — context matters.

f) STATUS AWARENESS: Factor in the status column:
   - "Added"    → already a keyword; flag if it should not be
   - "Excluded" → already a negative; confirm whether the exclusion is correct
   - "None"     → no action taken; this is your primary analysis target

g) EXPERT INTUITION: Apply real-world PPC knowledge. Some terms look harmless but
   consistently attract the wrong audience. Some look unusual but are high-value
   niche queries. Think beyond the raw data.

DO NOT:
- Base relevancy solely on impressions, clicks, or cost
- Mark a term "Directly Relevant" just because it has conversions if it is
  semantically misaligned with the business
- Mark a term "Directly Irrelevant" just because it has no clicks if it is
  clearly on-topic
- Suggest excluding a "Directly Relevant" term just because performance is low
- Suggest broad-match exclusions for specific, high-value terms

-- SECTION 3: RELEVANCY SCALE --

Assign exactly one of the following five labels to each search term:

Directly Relevant
  The term precisely matches the business's offering, target audience, and
  campaign intent. High confidence this user is an ideal customer.

Indirectly Relevant
  The term is thematically related and may attract a partially qualified audience.
  The user could be a potential customer but the signal is weak or imprecise.
  Monitor closely.

Borderline
  The term is ambiguous. Part of the audience searching this could be relevant,
  part could not. Intent is unclear or mixed. Requires manual judgment or further
  data before deciding.

Indirectly Irrelevant
  The term is topically adjacent but the user's intent, context, or profile makes
  them a poor fit. Examples: wrong funnel stage, wrong geography, wrong use case,
  DIY intent vs. professional service.

Directly Irrelevant
  The term has no meaningful connection to the business, its offerings, or its
  target audience. The ad should never have shown for this query.

-- SECTION 4: EXCLUSION SUGGESTION RULES --

WHEN TO SUGGEST AN EXCLUSION:
Provide an exclusion suggestion for every term where there is an existing or
potential relevancy problem — whether full or partial. This includes:
- All "Directly Irrelevant" terms
- All "Indirectly Irrelevant" terms
- All "Borderline" terms where a specific problematic modifier can be isolated
- "Indirectly Relevant" terms where a problematic word within the term is the
  source of the problem (partial exclusion)

Do NOT suggest exclusions for "Directly Relevant" or "Indirectly Relevant" terms
where no specific problematic component can be isolated.

FULL vs. PARTIAL TERM EXCLUSION:
- Full term exclusion: use when the entire search term is irrelevant and no part
  of it has value
- Partial term exclusion: use when only a specific word or modifier causes
  irrelevance; isolate and exclude only that component

MATCH TYPE SYNTAX:
- Broad match negative  →  no symbols       e.g.:  free
- Phrase match negative →  double quotes    e.g.:  "how to"
- Exact match negative  →  square brackets  e.g.:  [free accounting software]

MATCH TYPE SELECTION:
- Broad: short, universally problematic terms (e.g. free, jobs, salary, tutorial)
- Phrase: multi-word problems where word order matters (e.g. "how to", "what is")
- Exact: only when the term is problematic in this exact form but subsets of it
  could be valuable

EXCLUSION LEVEL:
- Account Level  : universally irrelevant to the business; add to global exclusions list
- Campaign Level : irrelevant to this campaign but potentially valid in another
- Ad Group Level : relevant to the campaign but not this specific ad group theme

-- SECTION 5: INCLUSION SUGGESTION RULES --

WHEN TO SUGGEST AN INCLUSION:
Provide an inclusion suggestion for search terms that represent a genuine
targeting opportunity — either as a new keyword to add, or as inspiration for
a new ad group or campaign theme. This applies to:
- Terms with "Directly Relevant" or "Indirectly Relevant" relevancy that are
  NOT already in "Added" status and show meaningful intent signal
- Terms that reveal an unaddressed audience segment or service angle that
  recurs across multiple queries (pattern-based opportunity)
- Terms that are "Directly Relevant" but currently matched by a looser keyword
  via Broad or Phrase match, suggesting they deserve their own Exact or Phrase
  keyword entry for tighter control

Do NOT suggest inclusions for:
- Terms already in "Added" status (they are already keywords)
- Terms with "Directly Irrelevant" or "Indirectly Irrelevant" relevancy
- Terms with clearly informational or navigational intent and no commercial signal
- Terms where the search volume is too low or the semantic match too vague to
  justify adding as a standalone keyword

INCLUSION TYPE:
Specify what type of addition is recommended:
- New keyword  : add this term (or a close variant) as a new keyword to an
                 existing ad group
- New ad group : this term reveals a thematic gap; create a new ad group
                 around this topic within an existing campaign
- New campaign : this term signals a distinct enough audience or service theme
                 to warrant a separate campaign structure
- Bid increase : the term already exists but is likely underserved by current
                 bid; flag for bid review rather than new addition

INCLUSION LEVEL:
Specify where the addition should happen:
- Account Level  : applicable as a shared theme across multiple campaigns
- Campaign Level : new keyword or group within this specific campaign
- Ad Group Level : add directly to this specific existing ad group

MATCH TYPE RECOMMENDATION:
Suggest the appropriate match type for the new keyword:
- Exact   [keyword] : for high-value, precise terms where tight control is needed
- Phrase  "keyword" : for terms with clear intent but natural query variation
- Broad    keyword  : only if the term is a strong seed for discovery in a
                      new theme with controlled bidding

FORMAT:
The inclusion recommendation is split across three dedicated output columns:

  inclusion_suggestion : The exact keyword to add, with match type symbols.
                         - Exact match  → [keyword]
                         - Phrase match → "keyword"
                         - Broad match  → keyword
                         Leave blank if no inclusion is warranted.

  inclusion_type       : New keyword / New ad group / New campaign /
                         Bid increase / N/A

  inclusion_level      : Account Level / Campaign Level / Ad Group Level / N/A

-- SECTION 6: OUTPUT FORMAT --

PART 1 — INFERENCE SUMMARY

State briefly what you inferred about the business from the dataset structure,
and note that these inferences informed all decisions below.

Output this section with the heading EXACTLY as: ## PART 1 — INFERENCE SUMMARY
Do not paraphrase, shorten, or alter this heading in any way.

PART 2 — SEARCH TERMS ANALYSIS TABLE

Output a complete table containing every row from the input dataset. Do not skip,
summarize, or omit any row. If the dataset exceeds 50 rows, output in clearly
labeled batches of 50.

Output this section with the heading EXACTLY as: ## PART 2 — SEARCH TERMS ANALYSIS TABLE
Do not paraphrase, shorten, or alter this heading in any way.

Column order — reproduce all original columns first, then append the new ones:

1.  search_term
2.  status
3.  campaign
4.  ad_group
5.  matched_keyword
6.  match_type
7.  impressions
8.  clicks
9.  ctr
10. cost
11. avg_cpc
12. avg_cpm
13. conversion_rate
14. cost_per_conversion
15. intent_signal
    Transactional / Commercial / Informational / Navigational
16. relevancy
    Directly Relevant / Indirectly Relevant / Borderline /
    Indirectly Irrelevant / Directly Irrelevant
17. relevancy_reasoning
    1–3 sentences explaining why this label was assigned. Be specific — reference
    the business context, intent signal, campaign fit, and any performance signals.
    Generic explanations like "not relevant to the business" are not acceptable.
18. exclusion_suggestion
    The exact negative keyword to add, with correct match type symbols.
    Leave blank if no exclusion is needed.
19. exclusion_level
    Account Level / Campaign Level / Ad Group Level / N/A
20. priority_for_exclusion
    High   = significant spend waste or brand risk; act immediately
    Medium = moderate risk; address in next optimization cycle
    Low    = minor issue; low volume or spend; address when convenient
    N/A    = no exclusion needed
21. inclusion_suggestion
    The exact target keyword to add, with correct match type symbols. Leave blank if no inclusion is needed.
22. inclusion_type
    New keyword / New ad group / New campaign / Bid increase / N/A
23. inclusion_level
    Account Level / Campaign Level / Ad Group Level / N/A

PART 3 — RECOMMEDNED GLOBAL EXCLUSIONS

Output this section with the heading EXACTLY as: ## PART 3 — RECOMMENDED GLOBAL EXCLUSIONS
Do not paraphrase, shorten, or alter this heading in any way.

This list contains broad and phrase match terms that are universally or
near-universally irrelevant to this business across all or most campaigns.
Typical candidates:
- Short, high-frequency terms that trigger irrelevant queries at scale
  (e.g. free, cheap, diy)
- Informational modifiers that signal zero purchase intent
  (e.g. "what is", "how to", definition, tutorial)
- Job seeker terms (e.g. jobs, salary, career) if not applicable
- Wrong audience or wrong industry terms
- Competitor signals where appropriate

Format:

| Negative Keyword | Match Type | Rationale |

Organize into groups:
  Group 1: Informational / Zero-Intent Modifiers
  Group 2: Price / Deal Seeking Terms
  Group 3: DIY / Self-Service Terms (if applicable)
  Group 4: Job Seeker / Employment Terms (if applicable)
  Group 5: Wrong Audience / Wrong Industry Terms
  Group 6: Brand / Competitor Terms (if applicable)
  Group 7: Other Globally Problematic Terms

Only include terms that appear as a genuine problem pattern in the dataset or are
clearly applicable global exclusions for this business type. Do not pad the list
with generic suggestions that do not apply.

-- SECTION 7: QUALITY STANDARDS --

1. COMPLETENESS: Process every single row. The output table must have exactly as
   many data rows as the input.

2. CONSISTENCY: Apply the relevancy scale and exclusion rules consistently.
   Similar terms in similar contexts should receive similar labels unless there is
   a documented reason for the difference.

3. SPECIFICITY: Relevancy reasoning must reference the specific search term,
   business context, intent, and campaign fit. Generic explanations are not
   acceptable.

4. PROPORTIONALITY: Match exclusion aggressiveness to actual risk. Do not exclude
   broad terms that would also block relevant traffic. Do not under-exclude when
   there is clear evidence of waste.

5. DATA INTEGRITY: Reproduce all original column values exactly as provided.
   Do not modify, round, or reformat any metric values.

6. AMBIGUITY: When genuinely uncertain, default to "Borderline" rather than
   guessing. Explain the ambiguity clearly in the reasoning column.

7. LANGUAGE: Analyze and respond in the same language as the search terms.
   If multiple languages are present in the dataset, note this and process each
   term in its own language context.

8. NO HALLUCINATION: Do not invent performance data, campaign names, or business
   details. Work strictly with what is provided in the input.

documentId.value: ={{ $('Create spreadsheet for storing analyzed search terms').item.json.spreadsheetId }}

parameters.content:
## AI Google Ads Search Terms Analyzer

This workflow connects directly to your Google Ads account, pulls the last 30 days of search term data, and uses an AI model to analyze every term — assessing relevance, identifying wasted spend, and generating structured exclusion and inclusion recommendations — all saved automatically to Google Sheets.

### How it works

1. **Trigger** — Runs automatically every 30 days (or manually on demand)
2. **Configure** — Sets your Google Ads IDs and business context in the Set node
3. **Fetch** — Pulls search terms via Google Ads API (GAQL searchStream)
4. **Clean & Format** — Normalizes and filters raw API data for AI processing
5. **Analyze** — AI model performs deep search terms relevancy analysis, gives exclusion/inclusion suggestions, generates a global negative keyword list and inference summary
6. **Save** — Results are written to a new Google Spreadsheet with 3 sheets: Search Terms Analysis, Global Exclusions List, and Inference Summary

### Requirements

- n8n (cloud or self-hosted)
- Google Ads account with API access and a Developer Token
- Google Ads OAuth 2.0 credentials
- OpenAI API key (GPT-4.1-mini or higher recommended)
- Google account with Sheets access

### Before you run

Connect your credentials in n8n:
- **Google Ads OAuth 2.0** → used in the HTTP Request node
- **OpenAI API** → used in the AI model node
- **Google Sheets OAuth 2.0** → used in all Sheets nodes

Update the **Set Google Ads IDs & business context** node with:
- `google_ads_mcc` — your MCC (manager) account ID *(if accessing the account directly, leave this blank)*
- `google_ads_customer` — your Google Ads Customer ID
- `google_ads_developer_token` — your Google Ads Developer Token
- Business context fields: company name, website, industry, company overview, target audience, campaign goals, key offerings, known irrelevant topics

### Additional customization

- **Change the AI model** — swap GPT-5.4-mini for Claude, Gemini, or any LLM supported by n8n
- **Adjust the date range** — modify the `DURING LAST_30_DAYS` clause in the GAQL query to use a custom range (e.g. last 7 days, last quarter, current month)
- **Change the schedule trigger interval** — edit the trigger interval to run the workflow weekly, monthly, or on any custom schedule
- **Change the metrics threshold** — the Clean & Format node has a metrics filter that excludes terms with fewer than 5 impressions or 0 clicks; adjust the threshold to match your account's activity level
- **Add Slack or email notifications** — append a notification node at the end to alert your team when a new analysis is ready
- **Customize the AI business context** — the quality of AI analysis scales directly with the richness of the business context provided in the Set node; the more specific the company overview, target audience, and known irrelevant topics, the more precise the relevancy scoring
- **Connect to Google Ads for direct upload** — extend the workflow to push approved exclusions back to Google Ads shared negative keyword lists via the Google Ads API

## Step 1 — Configure

Update this node with your Google Ads credentials and business context before running.

**Required IDs:**
- `google_ads_mcc` — Manager account ID *(if accessing the account directly, leave this blank)*
- `google_ads_customer` —  Customer account ID
- `google_ads_developer_token` — API Developer Token


**Business context fields** — the more detail you provide here, the more accurate and actionable the AI analysis will be:
- Company name, website, industry
- Business description
- Target audience profile
- Campaign goals
- Key offerings
- Known irrelevant topics

## Step 2 — Fetch Search Terms Data

Sends a POST request to the Google Ads API (v23) using the `googleAds:searchStream` endpoint.

Fetches all search terms from the last 30 days with the following attributes & metrics:
- Campaign and ad group name
- Matched keyword and match type
- Search term status (Added / Excluded / None)
- Impressions, clicks, CTR, cost, avg CPC/CPM
- Conversions, conversion rate and cost per conversion


⚠️ This node requires valid Google Ads OAuth 2.0 credentials and a Developer Token in the `developer-token` header.

If you access the client account directly (not via MCC), remove the `login-customer-id` header.

## Step 3 — AI Search Term Analysis (GPT-5.4-mini)

The AI model receives the full cleaned search terms dataset + business context and performs a comprehensive analysis of every term.

For each search term, the AI outputs:
- **Intent signal** — Transactional / Commercial / Informational / Navigational
- **Relevancy** — 5-level scale from Directly Relevant to Directly Irrelevant
- **Relevancy reasoning** — specific, business-context-aware explanation
- **Exclusion suggestion** — exact negative keyword with correct match type syntax
- **Exclusion level** — Account / Campaign / Ad Group
- **Priority for exclusion** — High / Medium / Low / N/A
- **Inclusion suggestion** — keyword to add, with recommended match type
- **Inclusion type** — New keyword / New ad group / New campaign
- **Inclusion level** — Account / Campaign / Ad Group


Additionally produces:
- **Recommended Global Exclusions** — grouped negative keyword list for account-wide application
- **Inference Summary** — if business context was incomplete, the AI documents its assumptions


⚠️ Processing time depends on dataset size. Default timeout is set to 180 seconds.
You can swap GPT for Claude, Gemini, or any LLM supported by n8n by changing the model node.

## Step 4 — Save to Google Sheets

A new Google Spreadsheet is automatically created on every run, named:
"Search Terms Analysis & Keyword Exclusion/Inclusion Suggestions (Company Name — DD/MM/YYYY HH:MM)"

Three sheets are written automatically:

📊 **Search Terms Analysis**
Full analysis table — every search term with all original metrics + AI-generated columns (intent, relevancy, reasoning, exclusion/inclusion recommendations).

🚫 **Global Exclusions List**
Grouped negative keyword recommendations ready for direct upload to Google Ads shared negative keyword lists.

📋 **Inference Summary**
AI inference summary — documents assumptions made from business context fields and search terms dataset.

user.name: Zeljislav Petrovic
user.bio:
Full-stack digital marketer with a strong technical side. Background in PPC/online advertising, email marketing, analytics, and marketing automation (Zapier, HubSpot, Zoho...) - and lately I've been going deeper into building AI automations and agentic systems in n8n. 

I create workflows that solve real business & marketing problems and turn manual processes into systems that run themselves. If something can be automated, I always find a way to do it.
